coredata.dc:title: Sunlight mediated enhanced photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics in aqueous medium using silicon doped carbon quantum dots decorated Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Gandharve
Cilamkoti, Vatsala
Dutta, Raj Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Residual antibiotics in water bodies, including drinking water has led to developing strategies for their removal. Here we report a very efficient method of photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and ciprofloxacin in an aqueous medium using an optimized batch of silicon doped carbon quantum dot decorated on bismuth molybdate (1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6) nanocomposite as a solar photocatalyst. The photocatalyst is synthesized by the hydrothermal route. Its structural, compositional, morphological, and optical properties are thoroughly characterized. About 97% of tetracycline (TC) and 95% of ciprofloxacin (CIP) are degraded in 90 min of sunlight exposure with a photocatalyst dose of 60 mg per 100 mL, which corresponded to a degradation rate of 0.023 min−1 and 0.025 min−1, respectively. The total organic carbon estimation in the reaction medium after photocatalytic degradation revealed 87.1% and 80.2% mineralization of TC and CIP, respectively. The photocatalytic degradation is attributed to the in-situ generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), e.g., superoxide radicals, hydroxide radicals, and the role of ROS towards the degradation of tetracycline and ciprofloxacin is confirmed from ROS scavenging studies. The degradation mechanism has been discussed by analyzing the degradation products via ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled to quadrupole-time-of-flight mass spectrometry (UPLC-Q-Tof-MS). The photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and ciprofloxacin by 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposites is significantly enhanced as compared to Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst, attributed to interfacial transfer of a photogenerated electron from Bi2MoO6 to SiCQDs, leading to effective charge separation of Bi2MoO6 and the charge carrier mobility.

Low resolution transmission electron microscopy images of (a) 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, (b) Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes, and (c) SiCQDs; higher resolution transmission electron microscopy images of (d) SiCQDs and (e) 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs; (f) selected area electron diffraction pattern of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6.

Binding energy spectra of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposite obtained from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements of (a) survey spectrum; (b) Bi 4 f; (c) Mo 3d; (d) C 1 s; (e) N 2p; (f) Si 2p, and (g) O 1 s.

Tauc’s plot of pristine Bi2MoO6, and SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs.

a) Dynamic curves of photocatalytic degradation of TC by different photocatalysts, b) pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting of TC degradation over different photocatalyst, under sunlight, c) Dynamic curves of photocatalytic degradation of CIP by different photocatalysts, and d) pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting of CIP degradation over different photocatalyst, under sunlight irradiation.
(a) Re-usability of the 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs for five photocatalytic degradation cycles; (b) X-ray diffraction pattern and (c) scanning electron microscopy image of the re-used photocatalyst.

(a-b) Effect of reactive oxygen species scavengers on photocatalytic degradation of TC and CIP by 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs under sunlight, respectively, (c) steady state room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra, (d) electrochemical impedance spectra of Bi2MoO6 and 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs and (e) Mott-Schottky plot of Bi2MoO6.

Schematic diagram of proposed (a) TC and (b) CIP degradation pathway by 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs as photocatalyst along with their MS spectra depicting the possible intermediates of TC and CIP photocatalytic degradation by 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs.
Schematic diagram showing Sunlight mediated charge transfer and ROS generation in SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs towards photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and Ciprafloxacin.
Summary of the photocatalytic degradation experiments carried out under optimized condition, i.e., photocatalyst and sunlight.
Comparison of our study with some of the recent literature on the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline by various semiconductor type photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Sunlight mediated enhanced photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics in aqueous medium using silicon doped carbon quantum dots decorated Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Residual antibiotics in water bodies, including drinking water has led to developing strategies for their removal. Here we report a very efficient method of photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and ciprofloxacin in an aqueous medium using an optimized batch of silicon doped carbon quantum dot decorated on bismuth molybdate (1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6) nanocomposite as a solar photocatalyst. The photocatalyst is synthesized by the hydrothermal route. Its structural, compositional, morphological, and optical properties are thoroughly characterized. About 97% of tetracycline (TC) and 95% of ciprofloxacin (CIP) are degraded in 90 min of sunlight exposure with a photocatalyst dose of 60 mg per 100 mL, which corresponded to a degradation rate of 0.023 min−1 and 0.025 min−1, respectively. The total organic carbon estimation in the reaction medium after photocatalytic degradation revealed 87.1% and 80.2% mineralization of TC and CIP, respectively. The photocatalytic degradation is attributed to the in-situ generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), e.g., superoxide radicals, hydroxide radicals, and the role of ROS towards the degradation of tetracycline and ciprofloxacin is confirmed from ROS scavenging studies. The degradation mechanism has been discussed by analyzing the degradation products via ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled to quadrupole-time-of-flight mass spectrometry (UPLC-Q-Tof-MS). The photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and ciprofloxacin by 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposites is significantly enhanced as compared to Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst, attributed to interfacial transfer of a photogenerated electron from Bi2MoO6 to SiCQDs, leading to effective charge separation of Bi2MoO6 and the charge carrier mobility.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) in aquatic environments are recognized as significant emerging contaminants owning to their widespread usage and subsequent environmental pollution [1–3]. Various water sources are contaminated by residual antibiotics, which enter the environment through multiple means like leaching from manufacturing plants, hospitals wastewater, mammalian discharge through urine and faeces, etc. [4,5]. Tetracycline (TC) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) are widely used for the treatment of various diseases in mammals due to their high antibacterial property and low cost [6–9]. However, the residual drug and faintly metabolized counterpart can easily toxify both surface and groundwater, causing chronic diseases in humans and animals due to the growth of antibacterial resistant strains within their bodies [10–12]. This has resulted in developing various techniques for their remediation. Among these, heterogeneous semiconductor-based photocatalysis has gained major attention from researchers for the mineralization of antibiotic-polluted wastewater [9,13,14]. However, most of the semiconductor photocatalysts suffer from limitations like intrinsic defects, poor redox properties, large band gap, and high recombination rate of the charge carriers [15,16]. Thus, designing an efficient photocatalyst that can minimize these limitations and show excellent proficiency towards TC and CIP degradation is quite challenging.

Though TiO2 and ZnO nanoparticles are well-established photocatalysts for the degradation of a wide range of organic pollutants, they mostly require UV light for photoexcitation due to their large band gaps [17,18]. This limits the use of economically favorable solar light-induced photocatalytic degradation, as only 6% of the solar light corresponds to the UV region [19]. Thus, there is a need for the development of photocatalysts, which can make use of the entire solar spectral energy, either by modifying the band structure of the existing semiconductor-type photocatalysts or by developing the new ones [20–22]. In this regard, bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6) is known to be a smaller band gap photocatalyst which is non-toxic nature and possesses good chemical stability. The photocatalytic activity of Bi2MoO6 has been improved by coupling with various semiconductor type materials e.g., WO3 [23], CdS [24], ZnO [25], BiPO4 [26], and by incorporating with carbon-based materials such as g-C3N4 [27], C60 [28] and carbon quantum dots [29]. Among these, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been very promising owing to their simple and cost effective method of synthesis, excellent chemical stability, biocompatibility and good electron conductivity [29–31]. In addition, CQDs serve as a photocatalytic center for absorbing solar light necessary for photocatalytic degradation and they are also reported to be effective in the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes at the junction interface [32,33]. Furthermore, conjugated structures in CQDs are advantageous to bind with organic pollutants having an electron-rich benzene ring via π − π interaction, ensuing rapid initiation of the photocatalytic process [34].

Recently, heteroatom doping emerged as a more convenient and general strategy to engineer the optical and electron transfer properties of CQDs. Nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) showed high luminescence performance and high electrical conductivity and optical properties, which accelerates the electron transport and produces good catalytic performance [35]. Subsequently, sulphur doping (S-doping) of CQDs is also highly beneficial for improving optical properties [36]. In this pursuit, literature suggests that silicon (Si) doped carbon dots towards enhancing photocatalytic degradation has been less explored [37,38]. Silicon is in the same group as carbon (i.e., group IV) but has vacant d-orbitals and prefers sp3-like bonding. Doping carbon quantum dots with Si might influence the electronic and structural properties of carbon-based material, and hence it is worth studying these effects on the photocatalytic performances [39].

Here we propose novel heterostructured nanomaterials comprising Bi2MoO6 decorated with silicon doped carbon quantum dots (referred as SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposites) as an effective solar-photocatalysts for antibiotic degradation applications. Such heterostructure is chosen as it is favorable for enhancing photocatalytic degradation owing to suppression of electron−hole recombination [35,36,40,41]. The optimized batch of SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposites has been studied for sunlight-mediated photocatalytic degradation of aqueous solution of tetracycline and ciprofloxacin antibiotics. The mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline has been discussed in the light of in-situ generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) and identification of degradation products by ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled to quadrupole-time-of-flight mass spectrometry (UPLC-Q-Tof-MS) technique.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (98%), sodium molybdate dihydrate (98%), ammonium oxalate, isopropyl alcohol, and chloroform were procured from HiMedia Pvt. Ltd., India. Trisodium citrate dihydrate, (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) were procured from Rankem Pvt Ltd. (India) and Sigma Aldrich, GmbH, Germany, respectively. Tetracycline (TC) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) were purchased from SRL, India. The ICP-MS (Inductively coupled plasma coupled to mass spectrometer) standards of Bi and Mo (Sigma Aldrich, Germany) were used as standards. All the experiments were done using deionized (D.I) water (Millipore 18 MΩ).

It is a two-step process, where silicon-doped carbon quantum dots (SiCQDs) were first prepared via hydrothermal route. Foremost, 0.5 g of tri-sodium citrate dihydrate was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, followed by the addition of 100 µL of APTES. Subsequently, the above solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h in a microprocessor-based temperature-controlled furnace. Finally, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The resulting solution of Si-doped CQDs was then precipitated with ethanol and recovered by centrifuging at 15,000 rpm for 5 min. The batch of Si-doped CQDs was washed several times with ethanol and then dried overnight in the oven at 40 °C.

Next, 2 mmol Bi(NO3)2·5H2O was dissolved in 10 mL ethylene glycol, and the solution was added dropwise to a 20 mL ethyl alcohol solution of 2 mmol Na2MoO4·2H2O during continuous magnetic stirring. After that, a definite wt% of SiCQDs (i.e., 0.5 wt%, 1.0 wt%, and 2.0 wt%) was added to the above solution mixture under constant stirring. The whole mixture was then transferred to an autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 24 h. The obtained precipitate was washed with deionized water and ethanol by centrifuging at 7000 rpm for 10 min, and the final pale-yellow product was dried at 60 °C overnight. The overall synthesis process is graphically represented in Scheme S1 (given as Supporting Information). The nanocomposites will be denoted as 0.5SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6, 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6, and 2.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 according to the wt% of SiCQDs precursor added. Likewise, a control batch of pristine Bi2MoO6 was prepared following the same synthetic protocol without the addition of SiCQDs.

The as-synthesized batches of SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs with different SiCQDs weight ratios, together with those of Bi2MoO6 nanoparticles, are characterized by powder X-ray Diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area technique, photoluminescence spectroscopy, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and by diffused reflectance spectroscopy. The details of these techniques and sample preparation are given as Supporting Information (Section S1). The total organic carbon (TOC) of the medium after photocatalytic degradation was estimated using the conventional protocol on a multi Analytik Jena N/C 3100 TOC analyzer (Made in Germany).

The degradation intermediates were identified by UPLC (ACQUITY I) coupled to QToF (XeVO G2-XS QToF). Mobile phase A was an aqueous formic acid solution (0.1%, v/v), and mobile phase B was acetonitrile. The gradient solvent was at a flow rate of 0.3 mL/min. The mass spectra (MS) were recorded in a positive ion mode using an electrospray ionization (ESI+) source under the following conditions: capillary voltage: 2.0 kV; gas (N2) flow rate: 50 L/h; gas temperature: 300 °C; nebulization pressure: 15 psi; scan time: 0.5 s

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed by using a standard three-electrode system (Metrohm Autolab B.V., Netherlands). The as-synthesized batch of the optimized photocatalyst (i.e., 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6) coated on ITO glass was used as the working electrode. 10 mg of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 was dispersed in 1 mL ethanol and subjected to ultrasonication for 45 min to form a homogeneous suspension. Then, 1 mL suspension was drop cast onto a pre-cleaned ITO glass substrate (1 cm × 2.0 cm) and dried in air. A Pt wire and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. An aqueous solution of Na2SO4 (0.1 M) was used as the electrolyte. The EIS measurement was carried out crossing the frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. The Mott-Schottky measurement was performed at a fixed frequency of 1000 Hz with 5 mV amplitude.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methodology for photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and ciprofloxacin
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activities of the SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs and the control batch of Bi2MoO6 NPs and SiCQDs as photocatalysts were evaluated by monitoring the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC, 20 mg/L) and ciprofloxacin (CIP, 10 mg/L) antibiotic solutions under natural sunlight irradiation. Typically, 30 mg of the respective photocatalysts were dispersed in the batches of 50 mL aqueous TC or CIP solutions taken in 250 mL beakers. This will be referred to as reaction assembly, which was subjected to slow continuous stirring (less than 100 rpm) for 90 min in dark conditions to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After 90 min the reaction assemblies were kept under natural sunlight (photocatalytic experiments were carried out at IIT Roorkee (29°51′N; 77°53′E) in the months of May and September 2021. The solar irradiance was assumed to be 221 W/m2 during the experiment day [42]. At regular intervals, 1 mL of the suspension was withdrawn from each reaction assembly and centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 2 min to discard any particles. The concentration variation of the TC and CIP solution in the respective supernatant was determined by recording the absorbance at λ = 357 nm and 276 nm by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan). The leached concentrations of respective constituents in the photocatalysts, viz. Bi and Mo from the batch of TC solution treated with 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs were determined by ICP-OES. The details of sample preparation are given as Supporting Information (Section S2). The photostability and reusability of the best performing photocatalyst, i.e., 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, was assessed by monitoring photocatalytic degradation of TC for five successive cycles. At the end of each cycle, the used photocatalyst was recovered, dried, and reused for the next cycle. After completing five cycles of photocatalytic degradation, the stability of the photocatalyst was studied by XRD and FESEM.

The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of SiCQD/Bi2MoO6 heterostructure is similar to that of pristine Bi2MoO6 ( Fig. 1). The characteristic Bragg reflection peaks for pristine Bi2MoO6 were detected at 2θ = 11.0°, 28.4°, 32.8°, 36.1°, 47.1°, 55.5°, 58.3°, 76.0° which can be indexed to (0 2 0), (1 3 1), (0 0 2), (1 5 1), (0 6 2), (1 3 3), (2 6 2) and (3 9 1) planes of orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS Card No.- 21 1272) (Fig. 1). The broad diffraction pattern with a peak near 23° found in XRD of SiCQDs (Fig. S1), is not reflected in the heterostructure as its concentration in the heterostructure was small. It also suggested that the incorporation of SiCQDs did not alter the crystal structure of Bi2MoO6.

The average crystallite sizes for pure Bi2MoO6 as well as 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposite are calculated using the Debye- Scherrer equation:
(1)
D =
0.9 λ
β Cos θ
where D is the crystallite size, λ is the wavelength of the incident X-ray (1.54 Å), β is the value of full width at half maxima (FWHM), and θ is the diffraction angle corresponding to the most intense peak, i.e. (1 3 1) plane. The crystallite sizes of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs were 1.3% less than the crystallite size of Bi2MoO6 (data given as Supporting Information, Table S1). This is attributed to broader XRD peaks for the 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 heterostructures, owing to the heterostructure-induced lattice strain phenomenon [43]. However, the proposed heterostructure induced lattice strain in 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs did not reflect any significant changes in the lattice parameters a, b, and c (given in Table S1). The lattice parameters were calculated from the inter-planar distance (d) and the miller indices (h k l) of the most intense (1 3 1) plane using the equation:

These results indicated that incorporation of SiCQDs in Bi2MoO6 did not change the unit cell crystal structure of Bi2MoO6, which implied that the SiCQDs are dispersed on the surface of Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes.

The morphologies of Bi2MoO6 and 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs were analyzed by electron microscopy. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) image of Bi2MoO6 NPs revealed agglomeration of nanoflake like structures ( Figs. 2a and 2b). Similar agglomeration of nanoflakes are also found in the FE-SEM of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs (Fig. 2c). The signature of SiCQDs in the nanocomposite is, however not convincingly observed in these FESEM images. The composition of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs is reflected from its energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum (Fig. 2d). It revealed the characteristic X-rays peaks of the constituents, e.g., Bi, Mo, Si, C, and N. Further information about the morphology of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs showing nanoflakes of Bi2MoO6 and spherical dots of SiCQDs are revealed from the transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The pristine Bi2MoO6 was about 40–60 nm long and about 10–15 nm wide ( Fig. 3b), while the TEM of SiCQDs revealed irregular spherical-shaped particles of sizes between 5 and 10 nm (Fig. 3c). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of SiCQDs revealed lattice fringes with a d-spacing of 0.256 nm (Fig. 3d), which is also found in the HRTEM of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs (Fig. 3e). It indicated the incorporation of SiCQD on Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes. In addition, lattice fringes of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs also showed an interplanar spacing of 0.314 nm corresponding to the (1 3 1) plane of orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 (Fig. 3e). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern confirmed the strong interference patterns due to the polycrystalline nature of SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs (Fig. 3f). The electron diffraction rings are indexed, which correspond to the orthorhombic structure of Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS card no. 21–1272).

The chemical composition of SiCQDs and 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 was studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The elemental analysis determined by XPS displayed that the SiCQDs is composed of 68.4% C, 27.5% O, and 4.0% Si. The detailed XPS analysis of SiCQDs was discussed in Supporting information Section S3 (Fig. S2). The survey spectrum of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 indicates the constituent elements, e.g., carbon (C), bismuth (Bi), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), silicon (Si), and molybdenum (Mo) ( Fig. 4a). The high-resolution Bi 4 f XPS spectrum revealed two strong peaks at 158.98 eV and 164.31 eV corresponding to Bi 4 f7/2 and Bi 4 f5/2, respectively (Fig. 4b). It confirmed the Bi3+ oxidation state in the photocatalyst. Similarly, the high resolution XPS spectrum of Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 were observed at 232.24 eV and 235.38 eV, respectively (Fig. 4c) [44]. This confirmed the Mo6+ oxidation state in Bi2MoO6 in the photocatalyst. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum revealed four strong peaks at 283.41 eV, 284.0 eV, 286.17 eV, and 288.16 eV which are associated with the C-Si, C-C, C-N, and CO bond (Fig. 4d) [45]. The N 1 s XPS spectrum can be deconvoluted into peaks at 396.35 eV (C-N-C), 397.98 eV (N-C), and 399.56 eV (N-H) (Fig. 4e). The Si 2p XPS spectrum revealed two peaks at 94.80 eV for the Si-C bond and 102.08 eV for the Si-O bond (Fig. 4f). The high-resolution O1s spectrum is fitted with two peaks centered at 530.64 eV and 529.73 eV (Fig. 4g), associated with Mo–O bond and the Bi–O bond, respectively. Therefore, the XPS results provide more evidence that the obtained 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 composite consists of SiCQDs and Bi2MoO6. Therefore, the XPS results are consistent with the XRD, CHN elemental analysis, and HRTEM findings shown above. This further provides more evidence that the obtained 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 composite consists of SiCQDs and Bi2MoO6.

The specific surface area of pristine Bi2MoO6 and 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs were analyzed by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, and information about its porous nature was obtained from the BJH pore size distribution plot (Table S2). The BET plot of pure Bi2MoO6 and 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs revealed type-IV isotherm ( Fig. 5), and the corresponding BET surface area was determined as 14.8 m2 g−1 16.5 m2 g−1, respectively. Their pore volumes were 0.08 cm3 g−1 and 0.09 cm3 g−1 for Bi2MoO6 and 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6, respectively. The growth of SiCQDs on the Bi2MoO6 surface increased the surface areas and the pore volume of the 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposite; which is evident from the high surface area of SiCQDs (shown in Fig S3 and in Table S2). A narrow pore size distribution between 2 and 4 nm is obtained (inset of Fig. 5), which suggested mesoporous nature of the 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs. The mesoporous nature is also corroborated from the Type-IV isotherm plot [23]. The high surface area and mesoporous structures of the 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposite are favourable for better interaction with pollutant molecules for adsorption and photocatalytic degradation.

The band gap of the as-synthesized batches of SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs and its constituents, e.g., pristine batches of SiCQDs and Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes, were determined from diffused reflectance spectroscopy studies. The pristine Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes and SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs strongly absorbs in the UV and visible light region (Fig. S4), while SiCQDs absorb only in the visible region (inset of Fig. S4). The diffused reflectance spectra were converted to absorption spectra using conventional Kubelka-Munk function [46], and the optical band gap was determined from the Tauc plots [47], given as.
(3) (αhν)1/n = A(hν – E g)

In this equation, α, h, ν, and A refers to the absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant, incident light frequency, and the proportionality constant, respectively. The value of ‘n′ is considered according to the nature of electronic transition, i.e., n = ½ for direct transition and n = 2 for indirect transition. The Tauc’s plots of 0.5SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, 2.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs and Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes are given in Fig. 6. The band gap of these materials was estimated by extrapolating the linear part of the respective Tauc’s plots to (αhν)2 = 0, as shown in Fig. 6, and by assuming the phenomenon of direct transition. The band gap of 0.5SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, 2.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs were determined to be 2.57 eV, 2.53 eV and 2.61 eV, respectively, which were similar to that of Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes (2.66 eV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC) and ciprofloxacin (CIP)
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline TC (20 mg/L) by the batches of SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposites (e.g., 0.5SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, 2.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs) and those by the individual components, e.g., Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes and SiCQDs, are given as a plot of C t/C 0 versus time ( Fig. 7a). Here C 0 and C t are the initial concentration and concentration of TC after a definite time of sunlight exposure. The decrease in the concentration of TC due to adsorption on the respective photocatalysts was first determined from the dark experiment. About 22% of TC was adsorbed on the batches of nanocomposites (i.e., 0.5SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, 2.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs), while about 16% of TC was adsorbed on Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes and SiCQDs (Fig. 7a). The higher adsorption of TC in SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs is primarily attributable to larger surface area and more favorable pore volume (i.e., 16.5 m2 g−1) than that of pristine Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes (14.8 m2g−1). The control experiment indicates that the self-decomposition of TC is negligible, whereas SiCQDs exhibited poor photocatalytic activity after 90 min sunlight irradiation. All the batches of SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposites clearly displayed significantly higher photocatalytic activity than that of pure Bi2MoO6 after the 90 min sunlight exposure, which indicates that SiCQDs plays an important role in the enhancement of TC degradation. The 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs manifested the highest photocatalytic activity (about 97% removal efficiency of TC after 90 min sunlight irradiations), while only 42% of TC was degraded under the same conditions using Bi2MoO6 as photocatalyst. The photocatalytic degradation mechanism was studied by fitting the time-dependent TC degradation data with a pseudo-first-order kinetics model. The first-order kinetics model is given as:
(4) ln(C
0
/C
t
) = k
1
t

where, C 0 and C t are already defined, k 1 (min−1) is the first-order photocatalytic degradation rate constant for TC ( Table 1). The corresponding photocatalytic degradation rate of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 (k 1 = 0.023 min−1) is 3.2 times than pristine Bi2MoO6 (k = 0.007 min−1) and 11.5 times with respect to SiCQDs (k = 0.002 min−1, Fig. 7b). The corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra recorded during the photocatalytic degradation of TC antibiotic solution by the optimized 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst under sunlight irradiation is given as Supporting Information (Fig. S5a). It can be seen that the intensity of the characteristic absorption peak of TC at λ = 357 nm decreased gradually, and it nearly disappeared after 90 min

Similarly, photocatalytic degradation of another class of antibiotic with different molecular structures, i.e., ciprofloxacin (CIP), was investigated by all the batches of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs and those by SiCQDs and Bi2MoO6 NCs. The photocatalytic degradation profile, including the initial dark experiments for CIP by all the batches of photocatalysts under study, are given in Fig. 7c. The extent of CIP adsorption on the different batches was similar to that observed for TC. As expected, the sunlight-mediated CIP degradation was significantly higher for the batches of SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs as compared to those by SiCQDs and Bi2MoO6. The best result, i.e., 95% degradation of CIP was recorded for the batch treated with 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs. The degradation followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Fig. 7d). The corresponding photocatalytic degradation rate for 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 (k 2 = 0.025 min−1), which is 5-times higher than the pristine Bi2MoO6 (k = 0.005 min−1) and 12.5-times higher than SiCQDs (k = 0.002 min−1). The corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra recorded during the photocatalytic degradation of CIP antibiotic solution by the optimized 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst under sunlight irradiation is given as supporting information (Fig. S5b). It can be seen that the intensity of the characteristic absorption peak of CIP at λ = 276 nm decreased gradually, and it nearly disappeared after 90 min

The degree of mineralization of TC and CIP antibiotics was estimated from total organic content (TOC) in the respective residual solutions treated with 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs after photocatalytic degradation for 90 min of sunlight exposure. Expectedly, these values were high than the TOC removal by pristine Bi2MoO6 (supporting information Fig. S6). The TOC values were 87.1% and 80.2% for TC and CIP antibiotics, which corresponded to 97% and 95% degradation, respectively. It may be assumed that longer sunlight exposure could lead to the complete mineralization of TC and CIP antibiotics into CO2 and H2O by treating with the photocatalyst [48]. It may be further remarked that our results on sunlight-mediated degradation of TC solution are better than or comparable to the photocatalysts reported in the literature ( Table 2).

The pathways for photocatalytic degradation of TC and CIP were addressed by identifying the tentative intermediates (represented as P) corresponding to the mass fragments analyzed by UPLC-Q-ToF-MS ( Scheme 1a and b). The possible degradation pathways are proposed here. The TC molecules (m/z = 445) are reduced to P1 (m/z = 393), from which a hydroxyl group is removed to degrade to P2 (m/z = 301). The intermediate P2 (m/z = 301) was deaminated to give P3 (m/z = 280) and P4 (m/z = 186). Finally, P3 (m/z = 280) and P4 (m/z = 186) further disintegrate into smaller molecule P6 (m/z = 164) and P5 (m/z = 167) respectively, which on further development of reaction break into small molecules P7 (m/z = 112) and P8 (m/z = 86). We propose further degradation of P8 to smaller molecules, and eventually, it is mineralized to H2O and CO2. Our proposed pathway is based on UPLC-Q-ToF-MS measurements and total carbon content analysis.

Similarly, the CIP degradation products were monitored by UPLC-Q-ToF-MS. The degradation of CIP may occur on the piperazine ring and the quinolone moieties by hydroxyl radicals. The product with a mass-charge ratio (m/z) of 332 is CIP molecule. Regarding the m/z ratios obtained in positive mode, 9 structures were identified for samples degraded in the presence of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6. In Pathway I, OH• and O2 ¯• attack the cyclopropyl ring and make it open to generate intermediate P1 (m/z = 362) followed by the decarbonization in the N atom alkylamine to form P2 (m/z = 334). The intermediate P3 (m/z = 279) was formed by the decarbonization and removal of the hydroxyl group from P2 (m/z = 334), which further gives the P4 (m/z = 280). The emergence of P5 (m/z = 229) was due to defluorination and removal of a hydroxyl group from P4 (m/z = 280). The intermediate P5 (m/z = 229) on further decarbonization gives P6 (m/z = 173), which on the removal of hydroxyl groups from the product P9 (m/z = 139). The intermediate P7 (m/z = 169) and P8 (m/z = 128) are formed after decarbonization from P5 (m/z = 229), and finally, the intermediate P9 (m/z = 139) and P8 (m/z = 128) mineralize to CO2 and H2O. In Pathway II, the intermediate P10 (m/z = 340) was formed due to the ring-opening of quinolone, which on decarbonization give P3 (m/z = 279). In Pathway III, under the action of OH•, leading to the substitution of F by a hydroxyl group and formation of the P11 (m/z 330), which on decarbonization form the product P5 (m/z = 229). Subsequently, all these possible intermediates were able to be mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

The robustness of the as-synthesized batch of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs as photocatalyst was assessed from its chemical- and photostability. The chemical stability was determined from metal ion leaching during the photocatalytic degradation process. The concentrations of leached metal ions of Bi and Mo at the end of five photocatalytic cycles, determined by calibration method using ICP-OES analysis, suggested minimal leaching (less than 2%), as given in Supporting information Table S3). The concentrations of the leached metal ions in the reaction medium were determined from the respective calibration plots (Fig. S7). These results suggested excellent chemical stability of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6NCs as photocatalyst.

The photostability of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs as photocatalyst for TC degradation is reflected from the reusability of the photocatalyst for at least five successive cycles of photocatalytic degradation under optimized conditions. The degradation patterns are similar and the rate of photocatalytic degradation for all five cycles is nearly similar ( Fig. 8a). The stability of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs in the reaction medium was further confirmed from the XRD pattern of the used photocatalyst recovered after the 5th cycle (Fig. 8b). The lattice parameters of the used photocatalyst are expectedly similar (Table S4, given as Supporting Information). Furthermore, the morphology of the used 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs recorded by FE-SEM did not show any significant changes with respect to the pristine batch of photocatalyst (Fig. 8c).

The photocatalytic degradation of TC and CIP by 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs was monitored under optimized conditions in the presence of specific ROS scavengers, e.g., ammonium oxalate (AO, 1 mM) for scavenging h+ [49]; isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 1 mM) for scavenging OH• radicals [50]; and chloroform (1 mM) for scavenging O2 ¯• [51] radical, respectively. The ROS scavenging action was evident from the decrease in the rate of photocatalytic degradation of both TC and CIP ( Figs. 9a and 9b). The most significant decrease in the photocatalytic degradation rate of TC and CIP was observed for the batch treated with chloroform, i.e., O2 ¯• radical scavenger (given as Supporting Information, Table S5 and S6). This strongly suggested that the O2 ¯• radical played a very important role in the photocatalytic degradation of both TC and CIP by 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst. Besides, the decrease in the rate of photocatalytic degradation of TC by 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 in the presence of IPA (OH• radicals scavenger) suggested that the photoexcited electron and holes were also involved in generating ROS in the reaction medium. The ROS scavenging studies complemented the PL results of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 regarding the predominant generation of superoxide radicals due to inhibition photoexcited charge carriers for the batch of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs and consequential enhanced photocatalytic degradation of TC.

Broadly, photocatalytic degradation is initiated by the absorption of photons of energy greater than the band gap of the photocatalyst, followed by inhibition of charge carrier recombination [52]. Various factors are responsible for the inhibition of charge carrier recombination, out of which the two significant ones are the presence of defect states of energy lesser than that of the conduction band and mobility of charge carriers to the surface of the photocatalyst for ROS generation.

The photoluminescence spectra of pristine Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes, 0.5SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs, and 2.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs revealed two radiative transitions at λ = 463 nm and at 511 nm. The one corresponding to 463 nm is attributable to inter-band transition and due to trap state emission from oxygen vacancies, while the other at 511 nm is due to defect states (Fig. 9c) [52]. It is noted that the PL intensities of both these transitions were significantly decreased for the batch of SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs as compared to that of pristine Bi2MoO6 nanoflakes. The decrease in the PL intensity is correlated with an increase in the rate of photocatalytic degradation. These results indicated that the charge carrier recombination is inhibited in SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6, which is typically due to interfacial charge transfer in the heterostructure [53,54]. These separated charge carriers tend to diffuse to the surface of the photocatalyst and generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) by reacting with molecular oxygen and water [55].

The mobility of the charge carriers in Bi2MoO6 and the batch of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 NCs showing optimum photocatalytic results was studied from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. From the respective Nyquist plots, the radius of the arc for the electrode made of 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 was smaller than the one made of Bi2MoO6 (Fig. 9d). Arc with a smaller radius is attributed to lower impedance or higher charge carrier transport [56,57]. Furthermore, the diffusion current, corresponding to the slope of the mid-frequency region (i.e., Warburg region), is more for 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 than in pristine Bi2MoO6. These results are complimented higher photocatalytic activity by 1.0SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6.

The interfacial charge transfer in a heterogeneous photocatalyst would depend on the favorable configuration of the conduction band and valence band energies in the photocatalyst. The Mott-Schottky plots were applied to determine their flat band potential (E fb) of Bi2MoO6, as shown in Fig. 9e. The E fb (vs. Ag/AgCl) of Bi2MoO6 is estimated to be − 0.71 V; whereas the E fb (vs. NHE) is − 0.49 V, calculated from the equation:
(5)
E NHE = E Ag/ AgCl + E 0 Ag/AgCl (0·22 V)

It is well recognized that the bottom of conduction band potential (E CB) of n-type semiconductors is located very close to Efb [34]. Therefore, the calculated E CB for the Bi2MoO6 is assigned as − 0.49 V vs. NHE. The valence band potential (E VB) of Bi2MoO6 is calculated to be 2.17 V vs. NHE determined from the expression: E VB = Eg + E CB. The Eg is taken as 2.66 eV for Bi2MoO6, as derived from the UV–vis DRS analysis (Fig. 6). Upon visible light irradiation, the electrons in the valence band are excited by the high-energy photon into the conduction band (CB). Silicon doped carbon quantum dots exhibit a synergistic role in the SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 hierarchical structure. The photogenerated electrons in Bi2MoO6 are quickly transferred to the SiCQDs because of their potential excellent electric conductivity [34]. As a result, the separation of photogenerated electrons from holes can be effectively promoted, and the degradation efficiency is further improved ( Scheme 2). The E VB of Bi2MoO6 (+2.17 V vs. NHE) is more negative than E°(OH•/H2O) (+2.4 V vs. NHE) [58], indicating that photogenerated holes cannot oxidize H2O to produce OH• radicals in Bi2MoO6. Nevertheless, the E CB of Bi2MoO6 (–0.49 V vs. NHE) is more negative than E°(O2/O2 ¯•) (–0.33 V vs. NHE) [59], which can easily reduce dissolved O2 to produce O2 ¯• which can degrade the structure of an organic compound by the free radical reaction. It can also generate hydroxyl (OH•) radicals due to chain reactions involving free radicals and subsequently take part in degradation. This was further confirmed from ROS scavenging experiment in the presence of isopropyl alcohol, which exhibited a decrease in the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TC and CIP.

It may be surmised that highly efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline and ciprofloxacin by SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst is due to better separation of charge carriers, better charge carrier mobility, and favourable redox ability of photogenerated electrons for production of ROS. Further studies may be carried out to implement the as developed SiCQD/Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst for treating real wastewater contaminated with antibiotics.

An optimized batch of heterostructure-based photocatalysts comprising 1.0 wt% silicon doped carbon quantum dots decorated on Bi2MoO6 has been synthesized by a simple two-step hydrothermal synthetic route. Compared to Bi2MoO6, the batch of SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposite exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity towards degradation of 97% of tetracycline (TC) and 95% of ciprofloxacin (CIP) under natural sunlight. The corresponding rate constants were about three times and five times higher than that of Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst, respectively. The improved rate constant has been discussed in light of the separation of the photogenerated charge carriers, confirmed from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements, owing to the formation of a heterostructure nanocomposite and sustained release of reactive oxygen species in the reaction medium. The photocatalytic degradation mechanism has been addressed by analyzing the degradation product by UPLC-Q-ToF-MS and from total carbon content. The excellent chemical and photostability and reusability of SiCQDs/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposite makes it an efficient photocatalyst choice for the potential degradation of pharmaceutical compounds in wastewater.
